blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْفَلَقِ (١)
Say, ‘I seek the protection of the Lord of the daybreak (1)
مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ (٢)
from the evil of what He has created, (2)
وَمِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ (٣)
and from the evil of the dark night when it falls, (3)
وَمِن شَرِّ ٱلنَّفَّـٰثَـٰتِ فِى ٱلْعُقَدِ (٤)
and from the evil of the witches, who blow on knots, (4)
وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ (٥)
and from the evil of the envious one when he envies.’ (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah al-Falaq

Some believe that this Surah was revealed in Makkah, although another group of commentators considers it "Madani" (revealed in Madinah).

The content of this Surah consists of teachings that Allah gives to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله specifically, and to other Muslims generally, regarding seeking refuge in His Pure Essence from the evil of all evildoers, so that they may entrust themselves to Him and remain safe under His protection from the evil of every harmful being.

Regarding the occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) of this Surah, narrations have been cited in most books of commentary stating that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was subjected to sorcery by certain Jews and became ill. Jibril عليه السلام descended and pointed out the location of the magical tools which had been hidden in a well. They were removed, this Surah was recited, and the health of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله improved.

However, the late Tabarsi and some other researchers have questioned such narrations, whose chain of transmission ends only with "Ibn Abbas" and "Aisha," because:

Firstly, according to the popular view, the Surah is Makkan and its tone matches that of Makkan Surahs, whereas the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله conflict with the Jews occurred in Madinah. This itself is evidence against the authenticity of such narrations.

On the other hand, if the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله could be so easily affected by the magic of sorcerers to the point of falling ill and becoming bedridden, he could easily be hindered from his great objectives. Surely, the Allah who sent him for such a magnificent mission and message would protect him from the influence of sorcerers' magic, so that the lofty station of Prophethood would not become a plaything in their hands.

Thirdly, if magic were to affect the body of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the misconception might arise among people that magic could also affect his soul, and his thoughts might fall prey to the sorcery of magicians. This notion would shake the foundation of trust in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in public opinion.

Therefore, the Holy Quran negates the idea that the Prophet was "mushur" (bewitched), stating: وَ قالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَسْحُوراً انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا: "The wrongdoers say, 'You follow none but a man bewitched.' See how they strike similitudes for you, so they have gone astray and cannot find a way!" (Al-Furqan 8-9).

Whether "mashur" here refers to someone bewitched intellectually or physically, in either case, it serves as evidence for our point.

In any case, the sanctity of the station of Prophethood cannot be called into question by such doubtful narrations, nor can they be relied upon in understanding the verses.

Regarding the virtue of this Surah, it is narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"Verses have been revealed to me the like of which have never been revealed: the Mu'awwidhatayn (the two Surahs of seeking refuge): al-Falaq and al-Nas." [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah al-Falaq, al-Nas, and 'Qul Huwa Allahu Ahad' in the Witr prayer, it will be said to him: 'O servant of Allah, glad tidings to you, Allah has accepted your Witr prayer.'" [^2]

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 716; and Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 567.

Furthermore, in a narration from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said to one of his companions: "Do you want me to teach you two Surahs that are the best Surahs of the Quran?"

He replied: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah!" The Prophet taught him the Mu'awwidhatayn (Surah al-Falaq and Surah al-Nas), then recited them both in the morning prayer and said to him:

"Recite them whenever you rise and whenever you go to sleep." [^1] It is clear that these benefits are for those who harmonize their spirit, soul, belief, and actions with its content.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 716; and Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 567.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ (1) مِنْ شَرِّ ما خَلَقَ (2) وَ مِنْ شَرِّ غاسِقٍ إِذا وَقَبَ (3) وَ مِنْ شَرِّ النَّفَّاثاتِ فِي الْعُقَدِ (4)

وَ مِنْ شَرِّ حاسِدٍ إِذا حَسَدَ (5)

Commentary

I seek refuge in the Lord of the Dawn!

In the first verse, Allah commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a role model and leader: "Say: I seek refuge in the Lord of the dawn" which cleaves the darkness of the night (قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ‌).

"From the evil of all that He has created" (مِنْ شَرِّ ما خَلَقَ‌). From the evil of all harmful beings, wicked humans, Jinn, animals, evil events and occurrences, and from the evil of the carnal soul (nafs al-ammara).

"Falaq" (rhyming with shafaq) is derived from the root "falq" (rhyming with khalq), which originally means splitting something and separating one part from another. Since the black curtain of night is split at the time of the breaking of dawn, this word is used to mean the rising of the morning, just as "fajr" is also applied to the rising of the morning for the same reason.

Some consider it to mean all births and all living beings, including humans, animals, and plants, because the birth of these beings—which occurs through the splitting of seeds, pits, and the like—is among the most wondrous stages of their existence. In truth, at the moment of birth, a great leap occurs in that being as it steps from one world into another.

In verse 95 of Surah al-An'am, we read: إِنَّ اللَّهَ فالِقُ الْحَبِّ وَ النَّوى‌ يُخْرِجُ الْحيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَ مُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتِ مِنَ الْحَيِ‌ "Indeed, Allah is the splitter of the grain and the date-stone. He brings forth the living from the dead and brings forth the dead from the living."

Some have taken the concept of "falaq" even broader, applying it to every kind of creation and origination, because with the creation of every being, the curtain of non-existence is split and the light of existence appears.

Each of these three meanings (the rising of the morning, the birth of living beings, the creation of every being) is a wondrous phenomenon that points to the greatness of the Lord, Creator, and Disposer. Describing Allah with this attribute carries a deep concept and content.

In some Hadiths, it is also mentioned that "Falaq" is a well or a prison in Hell, appearing like a rift in the heart of Jahannam.

This narration might refer to one instance among many, rather than limiting the broad concept of "falaq."

The expression "مِنْ شَرِّ ما خَلَقَ‌" (from the evil of what He created) does not mean that divine creation has evil in its essence, because creation is synonymous with bringing into existence, and existence is pure good. The Quran says: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقَهُ‌ "He who perfected everything He created" (As-Sajda: 7).

Rather, evil arises when creatures deviate from the laws of creation and separate from their designated path. For example, the sting or sharp teeth of animals are a defensive weapon they use against their enemies, similar to a weapon we use against an enemy. If this weapon is used appropriately, it is good; but if used wrongly against a friend, it is evil.

Furthermore, there are many matters that we outwardly consider evil but are inwardly good, such as awakening and warning events and calamities that rouse man from the sleep of negligence and turn him toward God; these are certainly not evil.

Then, in explaining and interpreting this matter, it adds: "And from the evil of every intrusive being when it enters" (وَ مِنْ شَرِّ غاسِقٍ إِذا وَقَبَ‌). "Ghasiq" is derived from "ghasaq" (rhyming with shafaq). According to Raghib in al-Mufradat, it means the intensity of the darkness of the night which occurs at midnight. Therefore, when referring to the end of the time for Maghrib prayer, the Holy Quran says: إِلى‌ غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ‌. The interpretation in some lexicons of "ghasaq" as the darkness of the beginning of the night seems unlikely, especially since the original root of this word means fullness and flowing, and certainly the darkness of the night becomes full and overflows when it reaches midnight. One of the concepts associated with this meaning is "attacking" or "assaulting," hence it is used in this sense as well.

Therefore, the meaning of "ghasiq" in the verse under discussion is either an "attacker" or any "evil being" that uses the darkness of the night to attack. For not only do predatory and biting animals come out of their dens at night to cause harm, but wicked, impure, and vile individuals also often use the darkness of the night for their evil purposes.

"Waqab" (rhyming with shafaq) is from the root "waqb" (rhyming with naqb), meaning a hole or pit. Subsequently, its verb form is used to mean entering a pit, as if evil and harmful beings use the darkness of night and, by creating harmful pits, act to realize their vile intentions; or this expression refers to "penetrating."

Then it adds: "And from the evil of those who blow in knots" (وَ مِنْ شَرِّ النَّفَّاثاتِ فِي الْعُقَدِ). "Naffathat" is from the root "nafth" (rhyming with habs), which originally means spitting a small amount of saliva. Since this act is done by blowing, "nafth" has also come to mean "nafkh" (blowing).

However, many commentators have interpreted "naffathat" as "sorceresses" (naffathat is a feminine plural, and its singular "naffathah" is an intensive form of "nafth"). They would recite incantations and blow into knots, thereby performing magic. Yet, a group considers it a reference to tempting women who continuously whisper into the ears of men, especially their husbands, to weaken their iron will in performing positive deeds. Throughout history, the whispers of such women have brought about many fatal events, ignited many fires, and weakened many firm resolutions.

Fakhr al-Razi says that women, because of the influence of their love in the hearts of men, exercise control over them. [^1] This meaning is more apparent in our age than ever before, as one of the most important means of spies' influence over world politicians is the use of female spies who, with these "blowers in knots," open the locks of secret chests, become aware of the most mysterious matters, and place them at the disposal of the enemy.

Some have also interpreted "naffathat" as "evil souls" or "tempting groups" who, with their continuous propaganda, weaken the knots of decisions.

It is not unlikely that the verse has a general and comprehensive meaning that includes all of these, even the words of gossips and talebearers who weaken and destroy the centers of affection.

Of course, it should be noted that regardless of the aforementioned occasion of revelation, there is no sign in the verse that it specifically refers to the magic of sorcerers. Even if we interpret the verse as such, it is not proof of the validity of that occasion of revelation; rather, it is only proof that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله seeks refuge in Allah from the evil of sorcerers, just as healthy people seek refuge in Allah from cancer even if they have never been afflicted by it.

In the final verse of this Surah, He says: "And from the evil of every envier when he envies" (وَ مِنْ شَرِّ حاسِدٍ إِذا حَسَدَ). This verse shows that envy (hasad) is among the worst and vilest of base traits, as the Quran places it alongside the actions of predatory animals, biting snakes, and tempting devils.

Key Points

1- The Most Important Sources of Evil and Corruption

At the beginning of this Surah, the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to seek refuge in Allah from the evil of all harmful creatures. Then, in explanation, it points to three types of evil: the evil of dark-hearted attackers who use darkness to assault; the evil of tempters who, with their words and evil propaganda, weaken wills, faiths, beliefs, affections, and bonds; and the evil of enviers.

From this summary and detail, it is understood that the bulk of evils and afflictions stem from here, and the most important sources of evil and corruption are these three. This is very meaningful and worthy of reflection.

2- Harmony of the Verses

It is noteworthy that in the first verse of this Surah, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commanded to seek refuge in the Lord of "Falaq" from the evil of all harmful beings. The choice of "Lord of the Dawn" is perhaps because evil beings cut off light, brightness, health, and guidance, while the Lord of the Dawn is the Splitter of darkness and gloom.

3- The Effect of Magic

In the first volume, under verses 102 and 103 of Surah al-Baqarah, we had detailed discussions regarding the reality of magic in the past and present, and the ruling on magic from the perspective of Islam, as well as how it takes effect. In those discussions, we accepted the effect of magic in general, but not in the form that fantasists and superstitious individuals speak of. For more explanation, please refer to that discussion.

However, the point necessary to mention here is that if the verses under discussion command the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to seek refuge in Allah from the magic of sorcerers or the like, it does not mean that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was actually affected by their magic. Rather, it is exactly like the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله seeking refuge in Allah from any kind of mistake, error, or sin; meaning that by using Allah's grace, he remains immune to these dangers. If it were not for Allah's grace, the possibility of magic affecting him would exist. This is one aspect.

On the other hand, as we said before, we have no evidence that "naffathat fi al-'uqad" specifically refers to sorcerers.

4- The Evil of Enviers!

"Hasad" (envy) is a vile satanic trait that arises in a human being due to various factors such as weakness of faith, narrow-mindedness, and stinginess. It means desiring and wishing for the removal of a blessing from another.

Envy is the source of many major sins.

As stated in narrations, envy consumes and destroys a person's faith just as fire consumes wood! As Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام says:

"Indeed, envy consumes faith just as fire consumes wood." [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned:

"The blight of religion is envy, conceit, and boasting." [^2] This is because the envier is, in reality, objecting to Allah's wisdom: why has He granted blessings to certain individuals and included them in His favor? As we read in verse 54 of Surah al-Nisa: أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى‌ ما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌.

The act of envy may reach a point where the envier throws himself into fire and water and destroys himself just to see the blessing removed from the envied person, as seen in famous stories and histories.

In the condemnation of envy, it is enough to say that the first murder that occurred in the world was by "Cain" (Qabil) against his brother "Abel" (Habil), driven by "envy."

"Enviers" have always been one of the obstacles in the path of the Prophets and Saints. Therefore, the Holy Quran commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to seek refuge in Allah and the Lord of the Dawn from the evil of enviers.

Although the addressee in this Surah and the next is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, the intention is certainly for him to be a role model, and everyone must seek refuge in Allah from the evil of enviers.

O Allah! We too seek refuge in Your Holy Essence from the evil of enviers.

O Lord! We ask You to protect us as well from envying others.

O God! Protect us from the evil of the blowers in knots and the tempters on the path of truth.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Falaq.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 237. [^2]: Ibid., p. 248.
